name: Mastitis (Clinical and Subclinical)
pathogen: Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus agalactiae, E. coli, Klebsiella
symptoms: Swollen hot painful udder; clots or flakes in milk; watery discoloured milk; drop in milk yield; fever in acute cases.
transmission: Bacterial entry through teat canal during or between milkings. Contaminated milking equipment, hands, bedding.
prevention: Pre- and post-milking teat dip (iodine). Dry cow therapy. Clean bedding. California Mastitis Test weekly. Cull chronic cases.
treatment: Intramammary antibiotic (cloxacillin, ampicillin-cloxacillin) for clinical cases. Systemic antibiotic if toxic. Supportive fluid therapy.
economic: High - direct milk loss + early culling + antibiotic withholding milk discard

name: Milk Fever (Hypocalcaemia)
symptoms: Wobbly gait then recumbent cow unable to rise; cold extremities; dry muzzle; staring eyes; rapid death if untreated.
transmission: Not infectious. Onset 24-72 hours after calving when calcium drain into milk exceeds mobilization from bone.
prevention: Pre-calving dry cow diet low in calcium. Oral calcium boluses at calving. Anionic salts in pre-calving ration.
treatment: IV calcium borogluconate (500ml slow IV) - life-saving within minutes. Subcutaneous top-up.
economic: High - untreated cow dies; producers who treat fast save cow + next lactation

name: Ketosis
symptoms: Drop in milk yield; weight loss; sweet acetone breath; depressed appetite; nervous signs (excitement or stupor).
transmission: Not infectious. Occurs when energy demand exceeds feed intake in early lactation.
prevention: High-energy ration through transition. Monitor body condition score. Avoid overfeeding dry cows.
treatment: Oral or IV dextrose (500ml 50% dextrose IV). Propylene glycol oral drench. B-complex vitamins. Improve feed quality.
economic: Medium - recoverable with fast treatment but production loss persists

name: Bloat
symptoms: Distended left flank; difficulty breathing; distressed animal; rapid death from respiratory failure if severe.
transmission: Not infectious. Excess gas production on lush legume pasture or grain overload.
prevention: Avoid lush clover/legume pasture without roughage. Introduce gradually. Feed hay before turning onto high-risk pasture.
treatment: Stomach tube to release gas; oral antifoaming agent (poloxalene); trocar emergency puncture left flank if life-threatening.
economic: High - bloat can kill high-value animal within hours

name: Foot-and-Mouth Disease (FMD)
symptoms: Fever; blisters on mouth, tongue, feet, teats; drooling; lameness; dropped milk; young animal mortality.
transmission: Highly contagious - direct contact, aerosol, fomites, contaminated feed/water. Spreads across farms and borders rapidly.
prevention: Strict biosecurity at border and farm. Visitor protocols. No importation from FMD-endemic countries. Surveillance.
treatment: No treatment. Mass culling and disposal if outbreak. Fiji maintains FMD-free status through Biosecurity controls.
economic: Critical - would devastate Fiji livestock industry and export eligibility

name: Anthrax
species: All livestock species + zoonotic to humans
symptoms: Sudden death with dark blood oozing from natural orifices; fever; swelling of throat/abdomen; cattle often found dead in pasture.
transmission: Spore-forming bacterium persists in soil for decades. Contaminated pasture, water, feed. Biting flies can spread.
prevention: Annual vaccination in known anthrax zones. Burn/deep-bury carcasses - do not open them. Quicklime disposal.
treatment: Penicillin G (high dose, early). Isolate contact animals. Vaccinate neighbours. Do not perform post-mortem in field.
economic: Critical - zoonotic risk to humans; mass mortality in herds; soil contamination long-term

name: Blackleg
symptoms: Sudden lameness; hot painful swelling on shoulder/hip/neck that becomes cold and crepitant (crackling on touch); rapid death.
transmission: Spore in soil enters via wound or GI tract; activates in muscle tissue. Often after heavy rain disturbing soil.
prevention: Annual clostridial vaccine (5-in-1 or 7-in-1 covers blackleg + other clostridials). Vaccinate calves at 3-6 months.
treatment: Penicillin G high dose (rarely successful once clinical). Vaccine is the real answer. Burn carcass.
economic: High - kills young best animals; easily prevented with clostridial vaccination

name: Brucellosis (Bang's Disease)
pathogen: Brucella abortus (cattle), B. melitensis (goat/sheep), B. suis (pig)
symptoms: Late-term abortion (3rd trimester); retained placenta; infertility; mild fever; reduced milk yield.
transmission: Infected placental tissue, milk, semen. Zoonotic - handlers contract undulant fever.
prevention: Test-and-cull program. Vaccinate heifers (S19 or RB51). Disinfect calving area. Pasteurize milk.
treatment: No effective treatment - cull infected animals. Handlers wear PPE. Pasteurize all milk.
notifiable: NOTIFIABLE - Fiji Biosecurity Authority (Fiji officially brucellosis-free)
economic: High - zoonotic + reproductive losses + export restrictions

name: Pink Eye (Infectious Bovine Keratoconjunctivitis)
symptoms: Excessive tear production; corneal cloudiness; ulceration; partial or full blindness in severe cases; weight loss.
transmission: Flies transmit between animals. Dusty conditions and UV light worsen. Young stock most affected.
prevention: Fly control. Dust suppression. Shade provision. Reduce stocking density in affected paddock.
treatment: Subconjunctival injection of oxytetracycline or LA tetracycline IM. Eye patch. Isolate affected animals.
economic: Medium - affects feed intake and growth; visual impairment reduces grazing efficiency

name: Tick-Borne Disease Complex (Babesiosis, Anaplasmosis)
symptoms: Fever; anaemia; jaundice; red urine (babesiosis); weakness; abortion; milk drop; high mortality in naive animals.
transmission: Tick-transmitted (cattle tick Rhipicephalus). Blood-to-blood via ticks or contaminated instruments.
prevention: Regular tick dipping/spraying (amitraz, deltamethrin pour-on). Vaccine available in endemic regions. Quarantine new arrivals.
treatment: Imidocarb dipropionate injection (babesia). Tetracycline (anaplasmosis). Supportive transfusion if severe.
economic: High - naive Fiji cattle imported to tick zone suffer heavy losses

name: Cattle Ticks and Tick Infestation
symptoms: Visible ticks on ears, dewlap, udder, tail area; weight loss; anaemia; hide damage; vector of babesiosis/anaplasmosis.
transmission: Tick attaches from pasture. Life cycle 3 weeks on host + weeks on pasture.
prevention: Rotational grazing with spelling period. Regular dipping every 21 days. Use resistant breeds (Bos indicus).
treatment: Amitraz dip or spray every 21 days. Deltamethrin pour-on every 28 days. Ivermectin injection (also covers internal parasites).
economic: High - production loss + hide damage + disease vectoring

name: Internal Parasites (GI Worms)
species: Cattle, goats, sheep (especially goats/sheep in wet tropics)
symptoms: Weight loss; pale mucous membranes (anaemia from Haemonchus); bottle jaw (submandibular oedema); diarrhoea; poor coat; sudden death in young.
transmission: Pasture-to-animal via larvae on grass. Wet season peaks larval availability.
prevention: Strategic deworming (3-month intervals in wet season). Rotational grazing. FAMACHA scoring for anaemia in goats/sheep.
treatment: Ivermectin injection (broad spectrum). Albendazole oral (flukes + worms). Levamisole alternative. Rotate dewormer classes to prevent resistance.
economic: Critical in goats/sheep - can halve stocking rate; Haemonchus kills naive young stock

name: Ringworm
species: Cattle, goats, sheep + zoonotic to humans
symptoms: Circular hairless patches with grey crust; typically on head, neck, shoulders; itchy.
transmission: Spore-contaminated fences, feeders, brushes. Self-limiting but takes months. Young stock most affected.
prevention: Disinfect equipment. Sunlight exposure of yards. Segregate affected animals.
treatment: Topical iodine or enilconazole wash. Self-resolves in 2-4 months. Handlers wear gloves.
economic: Low - cosmetic; zoonotic nuisance

name: Classical Swine Fever (CSF / Hog Cholera)
symptoms: High fever 41C+; huddling; reddish-purple discoloration ears/belly; nervous signs; diarrhoea; up to 100% mortality in naive herds.
transmission: Highly contagious - direct contact, fomites, contaminated feed (swill), infected pork products.
prevention: Vaccination in endemic regions. Strict biosecurity. No swill feeding without cooking. Quarantine new pigs 30 days.
treatment: No treatment. Cull infected herd. Vaccinate contact herds. Notify Biosecurity.
economic: Critical - can wipe out entire piggery; trade impact

name: Swine Erysipelas
symptoms: Diamond-shaped skin lesions (chronic); fever + sudden death (acute septicaemia); lameness; arthritis; endocarditis.
transmission: Organism in soil, water, pig carriers. Stress triggers outbreak.
prevention: Vaccinate breeding stock and growers. Good hygiene. Avoid stress.
treatment: Penicillin G injection (responds quickly). Supportive care. Isolate affected pigs.
economic: Medium - responds to penicillin; vaccine is cheap and effective

name: Mycoplasma Pneumonia / Enzootic Pneumonia
symptoms: Chronic dry cough; reduced weight gain; poor feed conversion; rarely fatal alone but devastating combined with secondary bacteria.
transmission: Airborne droplets between pigs. Carrier sows infect piglets.
prevention: Vaccinate piglets at 1 and 3 weeks. All-in/all-out housing. Good ventilation.
treatment: Tylosin, tilmicosin, or lincomycin in-feed or injection. Antibiotics reduce clinical signs; vaccine prevents.
economic: High - silent cost: depressed daily gain and feed conversion on every animal

name: Porcine Reproductive and Respiratory Syndrome (PRRS)
symptoms: Abortion storms; mummified foetuses; weak piglets; respiratory disease in growers; blue ears in acute phase.
transmission: Direct contact, aerosol, semen, fomites.
prevention: Strict biosecurity. Vaccination. All-in/all-out farrowing.
treatment: No specific treatment. Supportive antibiotics for secondary bacterial infection. Acclimatize gilts before breeding.
economic: High - reproductive losses plus grower mortality

name: Piglet Scours (Rotavirus / E. coli / Coronavirus)
pathogen: Rotavirus, ETEC E. coli, Transmissible Gastroenteritis
symptoms: Profuse watery diarrhoea; dehydration; death within hours in newborns.
transmission: Faecal-oral from sow/environment. Colostrum deprivation increases risk.
prevention: Ensure colostrum intake in first 6 hours. Clean farrowing crates. Vaccinate sows pre-farrowing.
treatment: Oral electrolyte fluids. Antibiotic (amoxicillin, neomycin) for bacterial causes. Keep piglets warm and dry.
economic: High - piglet mortality directly reduces sellable pigs per litter

name: Pig Mange
symptoms: Intense itching; reddened skin; thickened crusty skin on ears, neck, flanks; stunted growth.
transmission: Direct contact between pigs. Infected housing.
prevention: Treat breeding stock. Quarantine new pigs. Clean pens between batches.
treatment: Ivermectin injection (2 doses 14 days apart) or in-feed. Amitraz spray for housing.
economic: Medium - affects weight gain and skin quality

name: Pig Internal Parasites (Ascaris, Whipworm)
symptoms: Poor growth; rough coat; pot-bellied appearance; coughing from lung migration (Ascaris); diarrhoea (whipworm).
transmission: Oral ingestion of eggs from contaminated feed/floor. Eggs persist years in soil.
prevention: Strategic deworming at weaning and pre-breeding. Concrete floors easier to clean than dirt.
treatment: Ivermectin injection or albendazole in-feed. Fenbendazole effective against whipworm.
economic: Medium - silent feed conversion loss

name: Newcastle Disease (ND)
symptoms: Respiratory distress (gasping, coughing); nervous signs (twisted necks, paralysis); green watery diarrhoea; sudden layer drop to zero; 80-100% mortality in unvaccinated flocks.
transmission: Highly contagious aerosol. Wild birds and fomites. Live poultry markets major risk.
prevention: MANDATORY vaccination program in Fiji. Lasota / F strain live vaccines at day 1, day 14, week 6. Booster every 3 months in endemic areas.
treatment: No treatment for clinical birds. Slaughter and disposal. Vaccinate surrounding flocks.
economic: Critical - endemic in Fiji, unvaccinated flock will crash

name: Marek's Disease
species: Chickens (layers most affected, broilers less so due to short cycle)
symptoms: Paralysis of one leg or wing; grey eye (iris discoloration); tumours in organs at slaughter; progressive emaciation.
transmission: Dander and feather dust aerosol. Persists in houses for months.
prevention: VACCINATE AT DAY 1 IN HATCHERY (HVT or bivalent). Non-negotiable for layers.
treatment: No treatment. Cull affected birds. Ensure hatchery vaccination.
economic: Critical for layers - unvaccinated flock can lose 30%+ over lay cycle

name: Infectious Bursal Disease (Gumboro)
symptoms: Sudden mortality; watery diarrhoea; ruffled feathers; vent pecking; immunosuppression leads to secondary infections and vaccine failure.
transmission: Faecal-oral. Very hardy virus - persists months in houses.
prevention: Vaccinate at 14 and 21 days. Clean out and disinfect houses between batches.
treatment: No specific treatment. Supportive (electrolytes, vitamins). Prevent secondary bacterial infection with antibiotics.
economic: High - causes immunosuppression making bird susceptible to everything else

name: Fowl Cholera
symptoms: Sudden death; dark cyanotic wattles and combs; green diarrhoea; swollen joints (chronic form).
transmission: Rodents, wild birds, contaminated water. Stress and overcrowding triggers.
prevention: Rodent control. Fresh clean water. Vaccinate in outbreak areas.
treatment: Sulphadimidine in water. Oxytetracycline in water/feed. Vaccinate unaffected birds.
economic: High - can kill 50%+ in acute outbreak

name: Coccidiosis (Poultry)
pathogen: Eimeria spp. (E. tenella, E. acervulina, E. maxima)
species: All poultry (young birds 3-8 weeks most at risk)
symptoms: Bloody droppings (caecal coccidiosis); reduced weight gain; huddling; mortality. In broilers causes 30%+ feed conversion loss.
transmission: Faecal-oral. Oocysts in litter. Wet litter increases risk.
prevention: COCCIDIOSTAT IN FEED (monensin, toltrazuril, amprolium) from day 1. Keep litter dry. Vaccines available for layers.
treatment: Amprolium in water (Amprol 20%). Toltrazuril (Baycox) as single treatment. Supportive fluids.
economic: High - Fiji endemic, coccidiostat is standard in commercial feed

name: Mycoplasmosis (Chronic Respiratory Disease)
symptoms: Coughing; sneezing; nasal discharge; reduced egg production; swollen hocks (M. synoviae).
transmission: Egg transmission from breeders; airborne between birds.
prevention: Purchase from MG-free breeders. Tylosin treatment of breeders pre-lay.
treatment: Tylosin in water. Tilmicosin (Pulmotil) in-feed. Difficult to eliminate once established.
economic: Medium - silent production loss and secondary infection trigger

name: Egg Drop Syndrome (EDS-76)
symptoms: Sudden drop in egg production with no other signs; pale/soft/thin-shelled eggs; production recovers partially.
transmission: Vertical transmission (egg); horizontal via contaminated water; ducks are carriers.
prevention: Vaccinate pullets pre-lay. Separate chickens from ducks. Clean water.
treatment: No treatment. Vaccinate next flock.
economic: Medium - direct lost eggs over lay cycle

name: Infectious Bronchitis
symptoms: Respiratory signs; reduced egg production; wrinkled thin shells; kidney damage (some strains).
transmission: Airborne between birds; highly contagious.
prevention: Live attenuated vaccine at day 1 and booster.
treatment: No specific treatment. Antibiotics for secondary bacterial. Supportive warmth.
economic: Medium - impacts both broiler growth and layer egg quality

name: Avian Influenza (HPAI and LPAI)
symptoms: HPAI: sudden 100% mortality, cyanosis, nervous signs. LPAI: respiratory, drop in production.
transmission: Wild migratory birds major vector. Direct contact and fomites.
prevention: BIOSECURITY. Screened houses. Exclude wild birds. Mandatory reporting.
treatment: No treatment. Mass culling. Stamping-out policy. Vaccinate only if government-authorized.
economic: Critical - zoonotic + industry collapse risk if outbreak

name: Fowl Pox
symptoms: Wart-like lesions on comb, wattles, face (dry form); yellow plaques in mouth/trachea (wet form); reduced egg production.
transmission: Biting insects (mosquitoes) are main vector. Direct contact.
prevention: Vaccinate (wing-web) at 8-12 weeks. Mosquito control around houses.
treatment: No specific treatment. Iodine on lesions. Antibiotics for secondary bacterial. Recovery in 3-4 weeks.
economic: Medium - self-resolving but causes production loss

name: Duck Plague (Duck Viral Enteritis)
symptoms: Sudden death; haemorrhagic diarrhoea; drooping wings; photosensitivity; up to 90% mortality.
transmission: Faecal-oral from pond water, contaminated feed.
prevention: Vaccinate ducks in endemic areas. Avoid wild duck contact on open ponds.
treatment: No treatment. Cull affected flock. Disinfect ponds and houses.
economic: Critical - duck farm collapse

name: Duck Viral Hepatitis
symptoms: Sudden death within hours of first signs; opisthotonus (head arched back); high mortality in young ducklings.
transmission: Direct contact, fomites. Carrier ducks shed virus.
prevention: Vaccinate breeder ducks. Separate ducklings from adults.
treatment: No treatment. Attack rate up to 95% in unvaccinated ducklings.
economic: Critical - can wipe out entire hatch

name: Foot Rot
symptoms: Lameness; swollen heel bulbs; foul-smelling discharge between claws; separation of horn from sole.
transmission: Wet muddy conditions soften hoof. Bacteria enter and establish.
prevention: Drain wet pens. Regular hoof trimming. Footbath with zinc sulphate or 10% formalin.
treatment: Trim infected hoof tissue. Apply topical antibiotic spray or iodine. Systemic penicillin or oxytetracycline if severe.
economic: High - lame animal loses weight and cannot breed

name: Pneumonia Complex
species: Goats and sheep (all ages, young more severe)
symptoms: Coughing; nasal discharge; fast breathing; fever; off feed; death in severe cases.
transmission: Stress-triggered. Transport, overcrowding, sudden weather change.
prevention: Reduce stress at purchase. Gradual feed transitions. Good ventilation in housing.
treatment: Long-acting oxytetracycline injection. Tilmicosin. Supportive fluids and vitamins.
economic: High - pneumonia outbreak can kill 20-30% of young stock in weeks

name: Pulpy Kidney / Enterotoxemia
species: Goats, sheep (young on lush pasture or grain)
symptoms: Sudden death in best-conditioned animals; convulsions; green frothy diarrhoea; rapid post-mortem autolysis of kidneys.
transmission: Spore in gut; proliferates with sudden high-starch/high-protein intake.
prevention: VACCINATE. Clostridial 5-in-1 or 7-in-1 at 8 weeks with booster. Booster annually.
treatment: No effective treatment once clinical. Antitoxin only. Real answer is vaccination.
economic: Critical - kills best animals; fully preventable with FJD 1-2 vaccine cost per animal per year

name: Caseous Lymphadenitis (CLA, Cheesy Gland)
symptoms: Abscesses in lymph nodes (neck, shoulder, groin); rupture releases thick cheesy pus; internal abscesses cause weight loss.
transmission: Pus contaminates pens, shearing/hoof equipment. Enters through wounds.
prevention: Lance abscesses away from herd. Disinfect equipment between animals. Cull chronic carriers.
treatment: Surgical drainage with careful disposal of pus. No systemic antibiotic works reliably.
economic: Medium - chronic losses; carcass condemnation at slaughter

name: Contagious Ecthyma (Orf / Scabby Mouth)
symptoms: Crusty scabs on lips, nose, around mouth of lambs/kids; can spread to udder of dams; typically resolves in 3-6 weeks.
transmission: Direct contact. Virus persists on dry scabs for years.
prevention: Vaccinate flock in endemic areas. Isolate affected animals.
treatment: Self-limiting. Topical iodine. Tube-feed severe cases unable to suckle.
economic: Low-Medium - young can starve if unable to suckle

name: Varroa Mite (Varroosis)
symptoms: Deformed wings on emerging workers; weakened colony; brood death; colony collapse if untreated over 1-2 years.
transmission: Mite reproduces in sealed brood; transferred by bee drift and swarm.
prevention: Monitor mite levels with sugar shake or alcohol wash. Treat at threshold (3% infestation).
treatment: Oxalic acid (vaporization or dribble) in broodless period. Apistan / Bayvarol strips seasonally. Requeen from resistant stock.
notifiable: Not notifiable but Fiji status uncertain
economic: Critical - colony collapse without treatment

name: American Foulbrood (AFB)
symptoms: Sunken greasy perforated brood cappings; brown ropy larval remains (matchstick test); foul smell; spreads rapidly within colony.
transmission: Spore-forming bacterium persists in wax and equipment decades. Spread via robbing and contaminated equipment.
prevention: Inspect brood regularly. Never feed unknown honey to colonies. Replace old dark comb every 3 years.
treatment: BURN infected colony and hive completely. Notify Biosecurity. No antibiotic fully eradicates spores.
economic: Critical - loss of entire colony and contaminated equipment

name: European Foulbrood (EFB)
symptoms: Twisted discoloured larvae in open cells; sour smell; generally less persistent than AFB.
transmission: Contaminated equipment. Nurse bees spread.
prevention: Strong colonies resist. Requeen weak colonies. Hygienic practices.
treatment: Shook swarm method. Oxytetracycline under veterinary supervision. Destroy severely affected combs.
economic: High - chronic colony weakening

name: Nosema Disease
symptoms: Bees crawling at hive entrance; dysentery streaks on hive front; shortened bee lifespan; colony decline.
transmission: Spores in bee faeces contaminate comb and water.
prevention: Good ventilation; clean water source; replace old combs; strong colonies.
treatment: Fumagillin (where available) in syrup. Management focus more effective than drugs.
economic: Medium - slow chronic production loss

name: Chalkbrood
symptoms: Hard chalk-like white or grey mummified larvae in cells or at hive entrance.
transmission: Stress, cool damp conditions, weak colonies.
prevention: Strong colonies; remove dead larvae; requeen with hygienic genetics.
treatment: No chemical treatment. Management and hygienic queens.
economic: Low-Medium

name: Streptococcosis (Tilapia)
symptoms: Swimming in circles; exophthalmia (popeye); darkening; haemorrhages at base of fins; mortality in grow-out phase.
transmission: Water-borne; stress and high water temperatures increase susceptibility.
prevention: Reduce stocking density; maintain oxygen; reduce ammonia; vaccine available in some markets.
treatment: Oxytetracycline medicated feed (7 days). Reduce stocking. Increase aeration.
economic: Critical - can kill 30-50% of grow-out stock

name: Columnaris
symptoms: Saddleback lesions; fin and gill erosion; white cottony patches on body; rapid mortality in warm water.
transmission: Bacterium in water; opportunistic on stressed or injured fish.
prevention: Reduce handling stress; maintain water quality; lower stocking.
treatment: Oxytetracycline feed. Salt bath (short). Reduce temperature if possible.
economic: High

name: White Spot Disease (Ich)
symptoms: White pinhead spots on skin and gills; fish scratching against surfaces; respiratory distress.
transmission: Protozoan with 3 life stages. Only free-swimming stage susceptible to treatment.
prevention: Quarantine new stock. Avoid temperature stress.
treatment: Formalin 37% bath (25ml/m3) every other day x 3. Salt bath. Raise temperature to accelerate life cycle.
economic: High

name: Motile Aeromonas Septicaemia (Bacterial Haemorrhagic Septicaemia)
symptoms: Haemorrhages on body; distended abdomen; popeye; ulcers; rapid mortality.
transmission: Ubiquitous in water; opportunistic on stressed fish.
prevention: Water quality management; reduce stocking density; avoid injuries during handling.
treatment: Oxytetracycline in feed. Improve water quality aggressively.
economic: High

name: White Spot Syndrome Virus (WSSV) - Prawn
symptoms: 100% mortality within days; white calcareous spots on carapace; red discoloration; gut empty; prawns at pond edges.
transmission: Horizontal transfer via contaminated water, cannibalism, vectors (crabs, other crustaceans).
prevention: Only buy SPF (specific pathogen free) post-larvae. Disinfect pond between crops. Biosecurity fence. No cross-contamination between ponds.
treatment: NO TREATMENT. Emergency harvest if economically possible. Destroy stock and sterilize pond.
economic: Critical - complete pond loss; hardest disease in prawn farming globally

name: Yellow Head Virus (YHV) - Prawn
symptoms: Yellowing of cephalothorax (head); reduced feeding; mass mortality 3-5 days after first sign.
transmission: Horizontal transfer via water and vectors.
prevention: SPF post-larvae. Strict biosecurity. Pond isolation.
treatment: No treatment. Emergency harvest.
economic: Critical

name: Vibriosis - Prawn
symptoms: Red body; black spots on gills; reduced feed intake; slower growth; chronic mortality.
transmission: Opportunistic bacterium in pond water; stress trigger.
prevention: Maintain water quality; avoid overfeeding; use probiotics in pond.
treatment: Oxytetracycline medicated feed (where permitted). Water management is primary.
economic: High - chronic production losses

name: Monodon Baculovirus (MBV) - Prawn
symptoms: Reduced growth; chronic low mortality; opportunistic co-infection with other pathogens.
transmission: Vertical (through broodstock) and horizontal.
prevention: SPF broodstock. PCR screening of post-larvae.
treatment: No treatment. Management focus.
economic: Medium - slow chronic losses
